stories.title: Learning to Ride a Bike
stories.story:
When Hana turned eight years old, she watched all the other kids on her street riding their bicycles up and down the road, laughing and racing each other. She desperately wanted to join them, but every time she tried to ride her own bike, she wobbled and fell almost immediately.

"I'm just not good at this," Hana complained to her older brother Sami after her fifth fall in a row. Her knees were scraped and her pride was hurt even more than her knees. Sami sat down next to her. "Nobody is good at it the first time," he said. "Want me to help you practice?"

Every weekend after that, Sami held the back of Hana's bike seat while she pedaled, running alongside her down the street. Slowly, without Hana even noticing, Sami let go for a few seconds longer each time. She would wobble, but catch her balance a little better each week.

One Saturday morning, Hana pedaled forward and realized the street behind her was empty — Sami wasn't holding on anymore. She was riding completely by herself! She turned around, grinning from ear to ear, and saw her brother clapping and cheering. Hana had learned that some things take many tries, but with patience and someone who believes in you, you eventually get there.

stories.title: The Group Project
stories.story:
Mr. Adel assigned his class into groups of four for the big history project, and Karim was placed with three classmates he barely knew. On the first day, everyone had a different idea about what to do, and nobody could agree on anything. Karim worried the project would be a disaster.

One teammate, Farida, wanted to build a model of an ancient castle. Another, Zaid, insisted they should make a poster instead. The third teammate, Nour, hadn't said much at all. Karim realized that if they kept arguing, they would run out of time and fail together.

"What if we do both?" Karim suggested. "Farida and Zaid can build the castle model, and Nour and I can research the history and write the poster to go with it." At first nobody was thrilled with sharing the credit, but slowly, each person started doing the part they were actually good at.

By the deadline, their group had built an impressive castle model with a detailed poster explaining its history. Mr. Adel said it was one of the best projects he had seen all year. Karim learned that a good team isn't about everyone doing the same thing — it's about finding out what each person does best.
